_comment: Pre-processed PatientSummary for frontend demo mode. Load with 'Try Demo Patient' button. No file upload required.

summary.summary_narrative: Mr James Hartwell is a 58-year-old man with a six-year history of Systemic Lupus Erythematosus (SLE) complicated by Class III lupus nephritis, recurrent pericarditis/serositis, secondary Sjögren's syndrome, autoimmune haemolytic anaemia (AIHA), and antiphospholipid syndrome (APS). He was admitted acutely in October 2023 with a major flare involving severe renal impairment (creatinine 198 µmol/L, PCR 487 mg/mmol), pericardial effusion, and falling haemoglobin (9.2 g/dL). He received IV methylprednisolone and commenced mycophenolate mofetil. At November 2023 review his renal function has improved significantly (creatinine 142, eGFR 49, PCR 88 mg/mmol), though ongoing monitoring and gradual steroid tapering is planned.

diagnoses.name: Dyslipidaemia (steroid-related)
diagnoses.status: active — statin deferred until disease stable

medications.name: Mycophenolate mofetil (MMF)
medications.prescribing_doctor: Prof D. Okafor / Dr S. Singh

medications.name: Hydroxychloroquine
medications.dose: 200mg (reduced from 400mg pending ophthalmology review — >5yr cumulative use)

medications.name: Warfarin
medications.dose: Dose adjusted to maintain INR 2.5–3.5 (current INR 2.8)

medications.name: Omeprazole
medications.frequency: Once daily (gastric protection while on steroids)

medications.name: Hypromellose 0.3% eye drops
medications.frequency: As needed (approx 10x daily for dry eyes)

timeline.event: SLE diagnosis. ANA 1:1280, anti-dsDNA 187 IU/mL, low C3/C4. Started hydroxychloroquine 400mg and prednisolone 5mg.
Prednisolone successfully weaned. Disease in clinical remission.
Secondary Sjögren's syndrome confirmed on minor salivary gland biopsy (focal score 2). Started Hypromellose drops.
Three-week history of worsening fatigue, arthralgia, malar rash, and dry mouth/eyes. Presented to GP (Dr Patel).
GP visit. Dipstick: protein +++, blood ++. Referred urgently to hospital. Prednisolone increased to 40mg.
Hospital admission — Ward 7B. Creatinine 198, eGFR 34. ANA 1:640, anti-dsDNA 412. ECG: pericarditis. Hb 9.2, DAT+ve (AIHA). IV methylprednisolone 500mg x3 commenced.
Echocardiogram: pericardial effusion 12mm, no tamponade, EF 63%. Chest X-ray: left pleural effusion, cardiomegaly.
Renal biopsy. Result: ISN/RPS Class III lupus nephritis (35% glomeruli affected, full-house IF, activity index 8/24). APS confirmed (LA positive x2, aCL IgG 68 GPL).
Mycophenolate mofetil 1g BD commenced. Warfarin started for APS.
Discharged home. Prednisolone 60mg od, MMF 1g BD, HCQ 200mg BD, warfarin, omeprazole, calcium+VitD.
Bloods: creatinine 142, eGFR 49, PCR 88, Hb 11.4 g/dL. Significant improvement across all markers.

Rheumatology outpatient review with Prof Okafor. MMF increased to 1.5g BD. Steroid taper started. MRI brain + neurology referral arranged for new headaches. DEXA scan requested. Dietitian referral for borderline glucose.
Ophthalmology appointment booked — hydroxychloroquine retinopathy screening + Sjögren's assessment.

clinical_flags: ⚠ Borderline fasting glucose (6.4 mmol/L) + HbA1c 43 mmol/mol — possible steroid-induced diabetes developing. OGTT or repeat fasting glucose in 4 weeks recommended.
⚠ New persistent headaches (3 months) — neuropsychiatric lupus or APS-related cerebrovascular event must be excluded. MRI brain + neurology referral arranged.
⚠ Dyslipidaemia (LDL 3.6, TG 2.4, HDL 0.9) — likely steroid-related. Statin deferred until disease stable. Monitor.
⚠ Vitamin D insufficiency (38 nmol/L) — continue supplementation, recheck in 3 months.
⚠ Borderline elevated RVSP on echo (34 mmHg) — monitor for pulmonary hypertension given APS/SLE overlap.
⚠ Smoker (5 cigarettes/day) — significantly increases clot risk with APS and reduces HCQ efficacy. Smoking cessation referral placed.
ℹ Hydroxychloroquine >5 years — ophthalmology screening for retinopathy scheduled 15/12/2023.
ℹ DEXA scan requested — prolonged high-dose steroid exposure, rule out osteoporosis.
ℹ Warfarin INR 2.8 — within target range (2.5–3.5). Continue monthly INR monitoring.
ℹ Renal biopsy chronicity index 2/12 — some irreversible scarring present. Long-term renal function preservation is a priority.
